questions.question: What are repair attempts in relationship conflict?

options.text: Attempts to win arguments
Any statement or action that prevents negativity from escalating out of control during conflict — verbal ('Can we start over?'), nonverbal (gentle touch), or taking breaks
Avoiding conflict entirely
Blaming your partner

questions.explanation: Repair attempts are de-escalation bids during conflict: verbal ('Wait, can we start over?', 'I'm sorry — that came out wrong', 'This is getting heated, can we take a break?'), nonverbal (reaching for hand, softening facial expression), or taking physiological breaks. Gottman's research shows repair attempts are MORE important than conflict frequency for relationship success. Couples who can repair mid-conflict stay together; those who can't are at higher risk.

questions.question: What is physiological flooding and why does it prevent productive conversation?

options.text: Flooding is a myth — you can always think rationally during conflict
Overwhelming arousal (heart rate >100 bpm) where amygdala dominates, prefrontal cortex goes offline, and you lose access to empathy/perspective-taking/problem-solving
Flooding only happens to people with anxiety disorders
Flooding is just an excuse to avoid difficult conversations

questions.explanation: Physiological flooding = overwhelming arousal during conflict (heart rate exceeds 100 bpm, rapid breathing, tunnel vision, racing thoughts). Brain changes: amygdala (threat center) dominates, prefrontal cortex (rational thinking) goes offline. You lose access to empathy, perspective-taking, problem-solving, communication skills. Productive conversation is impossible when flooded — physiology must downregulate first (takes ~20 minutes). This is biological reality, not manipulation.

questions.question: What is the 20-minute protocol and why is it important?

options.text: All conflicts should be resolved in 20 minutes
When physiologically flooded, take 20-minute break (cortisol and heart rate return to baseline), self-soothe, then RETURN to conversation — critical that you return or it's stonewalling
Breaks during conflict are always stonewalling
20 minutes is arbitrary — any amount of time works

questions.explanation: Research shows cortisol and heart rate take ~20 minutes to return to baseline after conflict arousal. Protocol: (1) Recognize flooding early, (2) Request break clearly ('I'm flooded, need 20 min, will return'), (3) Actually take 20 min minimum, (4) Self-soothe during break (walk, breathe, distract — NOT ruminate), (5) RETURN to conversation. Returning is critical — 'I need a break' + never returning = stonewalling. Less than 20 min = physiology hasn't downregulated, immediate re-escalation likely.

questions.question: When does self-soothing/taking breaks indicate abuse rather than healthy conflict management?

options.text: Taking breaks is always healthy
When partner physically blocks exit, escalates when you try to leave, threatens consequences for breaks, or uses days of silent treatment as punishment (NOT self-soothing)
All withdrawal during conflict is abusive
Only physical violence counts as abuse

questions.explanation: Healthy self-soothing: 'I'm overwhelmed, need 20-minute break, will return' — partner respects break, both return to conversation. Abusive weaponization/blocking: partner physically blocks you from leaving, escalates when you try ('Don't you DARE walk away!'), threatens consequences ('If you leave we're done'), uses days of silent treatment as punishment. Healthy relationships allow both partners to self-soothe. If breaks are blocked or weaponized, this is control/abuse (National DV Hotline: 1-800-799-7233), not communication issue.

questions.question: Which statement accurately reflects research on repair attempts?

options.text: Repair attempts don't matter — only conflict frequency predicts outcomes
Repair attempts are MORE important than conflict frequency — couples who can repair mid-conflict maintain relationships; timing, delivery, and chronic negative sentiment affect success
Repair attempts always work in all contexts
Only therapists can facilitate repair

questions.explanation: Gottman's research: Repair attempts are MORE important than conflict frequency. Couples who can repair mid-conflict stay together; those who can't are at higher risk. BUT: Repair attempts fail when (1) timing off (partner already flooded, repair attempted too late), (2) delivery sarcastic/contemptuous, (3) chronic negative sentiment override (all bids interpreted negatively), (4) one partner refuses all repairs. When repair attempts consistently fail, couples therapy needed to address underlying sentiment or power dynamics.

title: Repair Attempts and Physiological Self-Soothing - Quiz
